steps.title: Identify the mechanism that needs support
steps.content: Gil de Zúñiga and Diehl claim NFM may reduce voting probability through an indirect effect. "Indirect" means NFM doesn't affect voting directly — it must first weaken some intermediate variable (political knowledge or interest), and that weakened variable then reduces the likelihood of voting. A supporting finding must establish both links of this chain.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
Test each choice against the two-link chain: NFM → weakens political knowledge/interest → reduces voting:
  • Choice A — 'NFM increases near major elections; people vote more in major elections': this is about election type and timing — it doesn't address how NFM affects political knowledge/interest or voting probability through those variables. ✗
  • Choice B — 'NFM strongly reduces political knowledge and interest; political knowledge and interest strongly correlate with voting likelihood': this establishes both links — NFM weakens the intermediate variables, and those variables drive voting. Exactly the indirect pathway described. ✓
  • Choice C — 'NFM shows little correlation with political knowledge or interest': this breaks the first link of the chain, undermining rather than supporting the researchers' claim. ✗
  • Choice D — 'voting likelihood increases with political knowledge; but the NFM-knowledge link depends on social network size': this introduces social network size as a confound and doesn't cleanly establish the indirect pathway; the first link (NFM → knowledge) is conditional and unclear. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
